description: Create the Python PoC WebSocket sender example and all documentation (telemetry interface docs + README update).
steps: Create tools/dms_ws_sender_example.py - a Python WebSocket server on ws://localhost:8765/dms that sends sample DmsTelemetryMessage at 10Hz. Simulate changing yaw/pitch/roll, gaze, drowsiness, distraction using sin-wave patterns. Use the 'websockets' package. Include instructions at the top as comments: pip install websockets && python tools/dms_ws_sender_example.py

Create docs/DMS_TELEMETRY_INTERFACE.md with: WebSocket endpoint (ws://localhost:8765/dms), full JSON schema example, field meanings, units, score ranges (0.0-1.0), how to run dummy mode, how to run live PoC sender, how real DMS PoC should publish telemetry, troubleshooting section, expected message frequency (10Hz recommended).

Update README.md with a new 'Live DMS PoC Integration' section including: Frontend setup (npm install && npm run dev), Python sender setup (pip install websockets && python tools/dms_ws_sender_example.py), WebSocket endpoint (ws://localhost:8765/dms), brief architecture explanation.
acceptance_criteria: tools/dms_ws_sender_example.py exists and is valid Python using websockets library
docs/DMS_TELEMETRY_INTERFACE.md exists with complete documentation of the telemetry contract
README.md contains a 'Live DMS PoC Integration' section with setup instructions
Python sender simulates realistic DMS telemetry data including head pose, gaze, drowsiness, and distraction variations
verification: Verify tools/dms_ws_sender_example.py has valid Python syntax: python3 -c "import ast; ast.parse(open('tools/dms_ws_sender_example.py').read())"
Verify docs/DMS_TELEMETRY_INTERFACE.md exists and contains key sections
Verify README.md contains the new integration section
findings: All three files created successfully. Python syntax validated with ast.parse(). Cannot run npm install/test due to INTEGRATIONS_ONLY network mode. README section placed before Architecture section for logical flow. Python sender uses sin-wave patterns with multiple frequencies (0.3Hz, 0.7Hz, 1.5Hz, 3.0Hz) for realistic variation across all telemetry fields.
